context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Concepts: The Question of Ethics

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Development Planners are often faced with having to compromise in reconciling demands from very different groups, and are exposed to (sometimes conflicting) sets of responsibility (e.g. personal, professional, authority). Within this context, what is an ethical compromise? What is the ethics of planners remaining "neutral"? What responsibility should influence their practice? What is the source of ethical values? What ethical framework governs a context of radical cultural pluralism, as in a Western planner working in non-Western countries?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m honestly pretty lost on this ethics reflection. The prompt feels super broad, and I can’t tell what it’s really asking me to focus on. Could you help me figure out what the main question is supposed to be?
exchanges.tutor: That's a fair observation — the prompt does cover a lot of ground. But before I help you narrow it down, what do you think is the central tension or conflict that runs through all those sub-questions? Read them again and see if there's a common thread.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is feeling overwhelmed by the breadth of the prompt. Rather than summarizing or breaking it down for them, I should use a Socratic question to get them to engage with the text themselves. I'll ask them to identify what they think the central tension is, which will help them discover the core theme on their own. Keeping it bite-sized and not scaffolding too much.

exchanges.student: Maybe it’s something about planners having too many responsibilities at once? Like they’re supposed to be professional and neutral, but also make choices that affect people, so I don’t get how those fit together. I’m not really sure if the main issue is compromise, neutrality, or culture.
exchanges.tutor: You're onto something interesting. Here's a question to push that further: if a planner makes a decision that affects people's lives, can they really be 'neutral'? What would neutrality even mean in that context?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified something real — the tension between neutrality and making choices that affect people. This is good intuition and worth building on. I should affirm the direction without confirming it as complete, and push them to think more deeply about why neutrality and responsibility might be in tension. I want them to arrive at the idea that neutrality itself is an ethical position, not an escape from ethics.

exchanges.student: That’s where I get stuck, because “neutral” sounds good at first, but also kind of fake? If they choose one plan over another, they’re already taking a side somehow. I think I need help sorting out what neutrality could mean without turning this into a huge abstract thing.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid insight — neutrality starts to look like a fiction when every decision has consequences for someone. So if neutrality is impossible, what does that mean for the planner's responsibility? Who do you think they're responsible to?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a genuinely good insight — that choosing a plan is itself taking a side. I should affirm this and push them to think about what follows from it for the planner's ethical responsibility, rather than letting them get lost in abstraction. This moves toward the core of the assignment.

exchanges.student: I’m confused because it feels like the answer is “everyone,” which is probably useless. The state, local communities, employers, donors, and my own morals all seem like they could matter, and then I can’t tell which one the paper should prioritize. Could you maybe help me narrow which responsibility is most important?
exchanges.tutor: I won't pick one for you — and actually, the prompt isn't asking you to rank them either. Think about this: what happens when those responsibilities *conflict*? That tension is exactly what the prompt calls an 'ethical compromise.' Can you think of a concrete example where two of those responsibilities might pull a planner in opposite directions?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is looking for me to tell them which responsibility matters most, which I should not do. However, their instinct that 'everyone' is too vague is correct and worth building on. I should push them to think about what happens when these responsibilities conflict, which is actually what the prompt is asking about. This will help them discover that the interesting question isn't which one wins, but how a planner navigates the conflict. I should be careful not to give the answer.

exchanges.student: Maybe like if a government wants a development project pushed through fast, but the local community doesn’t really want it or wasn’t consulted much. Then the planner could be responsible to the state professionally, but also to the people affected. I still feel like once I start there, I won’t know how to connect it back to the bigger ethics part.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong example — hold onto it, it's doing a lot of work. Now, when the planner faces that conflict, what guides their choice? Where do their ethical values come from — their training, their culture, universal principles? That's one of the prompt's core questions.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a concrete and relevant example — this is real progress. Now I should help them see that their example is already doing ethical work, and nudge them to articulate what ethical framework or values a planner might draw on to navigate that conflict. This connects their concrete example back to the broader ethics question in the prompt. I should not over-scaffold but just ask one focused question.

exchanges.student: Yeah, and that’s the part that makes my brain go in circles. If I say universal principles, that can sound Western or imposed, but if I say local culture, then what if local norms are unequal or harmful? I can’t tell if the paper is supposed to resolve that or just describe the problem.
exchanges.tutor: You've landed on one of the genuinely hard problems in development ethics — good. Now go back to the prompt. It uses the phrase 'radical cultural pluralism.' What do you think that phrase is pointing at, and does it suggest the paper should resolve the tension or engage with it critically?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has hit on a genuinely deep dilemma — the tension between universal ethics and cultural relativism — and is now asking a meta-question about what the paper expects. I should not answer the meta-question for them, but I can redirect them to re-read the prompt, which actually addresses this directly with the phrase 'radical cultural pluralism.' I want them to engage with that phrase themselves. This is good progress and I should acknowledge it.

exchanges.student: I think it means there isn’t one shared moral framework everyone agrees on, especially when a Western planner is working in a non-Western place. So maybe the paper doesn’t need a perfect answer, but I still don’t know what kind of thesis counts here, and I’m worried I’ll just ramble.
exchanges.tutor: You've actually built up a lot of material in this conversation — the neutrality problem, the conflicting responsibilities, the cultural pluralism tension. Based on all that, what position do you want to take? Try stating your argument in one sentence, even roughly.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly understood what radical cultural pluralism means and has also correctly intuited that the paper doesn't need a perfect resolution. Now they're anxious about structure and thesis, which is a legitimate concern for a reflection piece. I should not write or suggest a thesis for them, but I can ask them to articulate their own position based on what they've already worked out in this conversation. They've developed enough raw material to attempt a thesis themselves. I'll push them to try.

exchanges.student: I think my argument is something like: planners can’t really be neutral, so ethical practice means being honest about whose interests a plan serves and being accountable to affected communities, but also being careful not to assume one universal moral standard fits every context. Is that too messy?
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid argument — it's not too messy, it's nuanced. One thing to consider: your last clause ('not assuming one universal moral standard') is important but a bit vague. What does a planner actually *do* with that caution in practice? Answering that will sharpen your thesis. Now, are you ready to start drafting?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a genuine thesis that captures real complexity from the prompt — this is meaningful progress. It's not too messy; it's actually quite substantive. I should affirm its strengths honestly without flattering, and push them to think about whether it fully addresses the cultural pluralism dimension, which is the trickiest part. I should not polish or rewrite anything for them. We're also nearing the end of our planned exchanges, so I should nudge them toward writing.

exchanges.student: Sort of, but I still feel foggy about how to turn that into actual paragraphs. Could you help me see a simple structure, because right now it all feels like one giant blob in my head?
exchanges.tutor: You already have the ingredients — you listed them earlier. Neutrality, conflicting responsibilities, cultural pluralism, accountability. If you were explaining your argument to someone step by step, which of those would you introduce first, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to provide structure, which risks me doing their organizational thinking for them. I should not outline the paper for them. Instead, I can ask them to identify the key ideas they want to cover, which they've already discussed, and let them derive the structure themselves. They have all the ingredients — neutrality, conflicting responsibilities, cultural pluralism, accountability — and should be able to sequence them.

exchanges.student: I guess neutrality first, because that seems like the opening claim everything else follows from. But then I’m not sure if I go to responsibilities next or jump to the Western/non-Western part, and I keep feeling like either order could work so I freeze.
exchanges.tutor: Both orders can work — stop waiting for the perfect sequence. Commit to one and start writing. You can always reorder later. The important thing now is to get a draft on paper. What's your first sentence?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is freezing on a sequencing decision that doesn't have a definitive right answer — this is a classic overthinking trap. Both orders could work, and I should say so clearly to release the paralysis, then push them to just commit and start writing. We've been in a productive conversation and they have enough to draft. I should be direct here and push them toward action rather than continuing to deliberate.
